titleStmt.title: No. 347Memorandum of Conversation, by the Chief of the Petroleum Policy Staff (Eakens)

div.head:
No. 347
Memorandum of Conversation, by the Chief of the Petroleum Policy Staff (Eakens)

opener.dateline: [ Washington ,] June 24, 1954.This memorandum of conversation was prepared on June 29.

list.head: Subject:
list.item: Negotiation of New Group Agreements by Participants in the Iraq Petroleum Company.

list.head: Participants:
list.item: Mr. Charles F. Darlington, President, and Mr. Charles L. Lockett, Vice President, Near East Development Corporation

div.p:
Mr. Darlington stated that the purpose of their visit was to inform the Department about certain impending developments with respect to the negotiation of new group agreements by the participants in the Iraq Petroleum Company. These new agreements, which are five in number, are expected to be signed by Mr. Gulbenkian in Lisbon next week, thereby bringing them into operation. They have been under negotiation with varying degrees of intensity for two or three years. In addition to correcting certain technical and administrative provisions, which in practice gave rise to certain difficulties, the new agreements provide the machinery
for implementing the 1952 IPC agreement with Iraq. In so doing, the new agreements provide for the purchase and sale of rights to oil within Iraq by subsidiaries of the participating companies. In the purchase and sale of these rights, the subsidiary companies realize a profit between the border price of 94 shillings per ton and cost of 13 shillings per ton, which is subject to the 50–50 agreement.

One new feature in the new group agreements is the provision which is made for the disposition of surplus oil. Up to the present time there has been no provision for surplus oil. Companies were required to nominate five years in advance the quantities of oil which they were prepared to take and it was that duty of the IPC management to make the necessary investments and other arrangements in order to produce the nominated quantities of oil. It has been found in practice, however, that the management, acting conservatively and not wishing to be caught short, has always expanded facilities to a level more than sufficient to produce the quantities of oil which the participating companies nominated five years previously. Under the new group agreements, if excess oil is available and one or more of the participating companies wishes to take it, the terms and conditions under which such oil may be taken are now set forth.

Mr. Gulbenkian’s 5 per cent interest has represented a special problem from the standpoint of the other companies in regard to implementing the 1952 IPC-Iraq agreement. All of the participating companies plan to set up local subsidiaries in Iraq which will pay royalties of 12½ per cent on the oil which they take, and in addition, taxes sufficient to total 50 per cent of the subsidiary’s profit. In turn, the parent companies expect to receive a tax credit for the amount so paid the Iraq Government as taxes against the taxes due the country of their incorporation. Since Mr. Gulbenkian neither takes Iraq oil nor pays income taxes to any jurisdiction, there is no advantage to him in having any payments made to Iraq on a tax basis. At the outset he took the position that the whole 50 per cent of the profit which would be paid to the Iraq Government should be paid as royalty and that if payments were to be made in any other way and the other participating interests should realize any benefits therefrom, he should share proportionately in those benefits. Recognizing that Mr. Gulbenkian was in a position to block the other participating interests from going the tax route, those interests agreed in principle to share proportionately with Mr. Gulbenkian any benefits which they derived from going the tax route. One of the five new group agreements takes care of this problem.

Another interesting point which came out in the discussion was the position of Compagnie Francaise des Petroles with respect to tax credits. Under French income tax laws, there is no provision for a tax credit being obtained by a French company for taxes paid in a foreign jurisdiction. The French representatives made this point in the U.S.-French oil talks in December 1953, and went on to leave the impression that this put CFP at a disadvantage compared with the other IPC participants in the sale of Iraq oil. The NED representatives pointed out that far from being at a disadvantage, CFP has been the beneficiary of an arrangement for some time (it is believed since 1952) that is roughly as favorable to CFP as a 100 per cent tax credit to the other IPC participants. Under the arrangement, the French Government agreed to allow CFP to organize a North African company domiciled in Casablanca through which its interests in Iraq would be handled. The North African subsidiary of CFP, while not receiving a tax credit, will in fact, however, pay only a very nominal income tax, believed to be 2 per cent, to the North African Government, thereby discharging in their entirety all of CFP’s income tax obligation on its operations in Iraq.

A point involving IPC’s operations which heretofore had not been completely clear concerns the price at which the participating companies acquire oil from the Iraq Petroleum Company. It has always been provided that such price of acquisition would be cost plus 1 shilling per ton. The extra shilling per ton is a payment made by the participating companies to the Iraq Petroleum Company outside of Iraq and hence is in no way involved in the transactions of the participating companies or IPC with the Iraq Government. Mr. Darlington thought this was a device for ensuring that IPC paid at least a nominal income tax to the U.K.

The NED representatives described at some length the mechanism now being used by the participating companies in making their payments to the Iraq Government. The payments for both royalties and taxes are now made quarterly, 30 days after the end of the quarter, by the Iraq Petroleum Company, which obtains a receipt for such payments. Hence, both tax and royalty payments to the Iraq Government are more or less up-to-date and current at all times. The receipts for such payments obtained by IPC are turned over to the participating companies who, when their tax payments fall due in April of the following year, utilize the tax receipts in the final discharge of the obligations.
The NED representatives undertook to supply the Department with two copies of each of the five new group agreements when they had been finalized and put into effect.
